title: Artemis Astronauts Lose Contact with Earth
summary: The Artemis astronauts are flying far from Earth to the Moon. They usually talk to mission control in Houston, Texas, using radio and laser signals. But on Monday, they will pass behind the Moon, and the Moon will block these signals. For about 40 minutes, they will have no contact with Earth. This means they will be alone in space with their thoughts. Victor Glover, one of the astronauts, hopes people on Earth will send good thoughts during this time. Long ago, Apollo astronauts like Michael Collins also lost contact when they went behind the Moon. He felt alone but not scared. On Earth, places like Goonhilly in England use big antennas to track the spacecraft and help NASA.

keywords.explanation: the big round object that orbits Earth
messages sent through radio or laser
a team on Earth that helps astronauts
a big dish that sends and receives signals

questions.question: How long will the Artemis astronauts lose contact with Earth?
What blocks the signals between the astronauts and Earth?
What does Victor Glover hope people on Earth will do during the blackout?
What is Goonhilly Earth Station used for?

background_read: Space missions like Artemis send astronauts to explore the Moon. They use spacecraft to travel and stay in touch with Earth using technology. Sometimes, objects like the Moon can block signals, causing a loss of contact. This happened in the past with Apollo missions too. Astronauts experience solitude during these times, but they are trained to stay calm. On Earth, stations like Goonhilly help monitor the missions to keep astronauts safe.
Article_Structure: WHO: Artemis astronauts and mission control team
WHAT: Lose contact with Earth for 40 minutes when behind the Moon
WHERE: Space near the Moon and Earth stations like Goonhilly
WHY: The Moon blocks radio and laser signals, creating a moment of solitude

perspectives.description: Feeling alone but hopeful during the blackout
Working hard to track the spacecraft and ensure safety
